text: What work are you doing out of fear that you'd stop doing out of choice?
usageNotes: Best used Monday mornings or after a career conversation.

text: Notice which parts of your workday you'd describe to a friend with energy — and which parts you'd quietly skip over.
usageNotes: Works well mid-week as a gut-check.

text: The version of you from five years ago had a very specific dream for this stage of life. How close are you to it — and do you still want it?
usageNotes: Powerful on birthdays, work anniversaries, or after a performance review.

text: Look back at what you wrote about your work identity last time. What word or phrase catches you differently today?
usageNotes: Requires prior session data. Best used on repeat users.

text: Today, name one professional commitment you've been maintaining out of inertia. Just name it — nothing more required yet.
usageNotes: Low-stakes entry point for users resistant to big career reflection.

text: Where in your body does your current job live? Shoulders? Gut? Chest? What does that location tell you?
usageNotes: Pairs well with somatic awareness or burnout check-ins.

text: Before the week fills in, name one professional thing you want to protect — a conversation, a boundary, a block of focused work.
usageNotes: Monday morning ritual prompt.

text: If your career were a season right now, which would it be — and what does that season typically precede?
usageNotes: AI tailors response based on which season the user selects (spring/summer/fall/winter).

text: What accomplishment from the last year do you rarely talk about — and why?
usageNotes: Good for high achievers who minimize their wins.

text: Notice what you reach for when work gets hard: do you lean into the problem, look for someone to take it from you, or go quiet?
usageNotes: Effective during high-stress periods or deadline crunches.

text: You've been optimizing for something in your career that you've never actually said out loud. What is it?
usageNotes: Works well in longer reflection sessions.

text: Send one message today that you've been drafting in your head but haven't sent. The hesitation is the data.
usageNotes: Action-oriented — good for users stuck in avoidance loops.

text: On a scale from 'just paying the bills' to 'this is my calling' — where does your work sit today, honestly?
usageNotes: AI adjusts depth of response based on where the user lands on the spectrum.

text: What would you build, make, or do if no one in your life would ever hear about it?
usageNotes: Strips social approval from ambition. Good for mid-career reevaluation.

text: Rate your professional energy right now: not your output, but your actual fuel. What's burning, what's running on fumes?
usageNotes: Useful at the midpoint of a big project or quarter.

text: You've reflected on your work identity before. Has the word you'd use to describe it changed — or just sharpened?
usageNotes: Requires prior session data on career identity.

text: Name one professional conversation you need to have this week that you've been circling around. When will you have it?
usageNotes: Action-forward. Best Monday or early week.

text: Your résumé is a story about the past. What would the first line of your next chapter actually say?
usageNotes: Good for job-seekers, career pivots, or anyone feeling stagnant.

text: Who are you trying to prove something to through your career — and when did that start?
usageNotes: Deep prompt. Pair with shadow work or inner child themes.

text: Notice what you feel on Sunday evenings. That feeling is information — not a flaw, not forever, just data.
usageNotes: Sunday-specific or for anyone examining work-life tension.

text: What's more true right now: you're waiting for permission, or you're waiting for the right moment?
usageNotes: AI tailors based on which the user selects — permission vs. timing framing.

text: Describe your current role in one sentence — not your title, but what you actually do. Now describe what you wish you were doing. How far apart are those sentences?
usageNotes: Two-part reflective exercise. Best for users with gap awareness.

text: What skill have you been quietly building that no job description has asked for yet?
usageNotes: Surfaces latent talent — good for creative and unconventional thinkers.

text: Think of the last time work made you lose track of time — not from stress, but from immersion. What were you doing?
usageNotes: Flow-state identification. Works well in career-clarity intake sessions.

text: You named a professional fear in an earlier session. Has it shrunk, shapeshifted, or stayed exactly the same?
usageNotes: Requires prior session. Tracks evolution of fear over time.

text: What do you want to be true about your work life by the end of this month? Say it plainly — no hedging.
usageNotes: Good for first day of the month or after a goal review.

text: You have a professional default — a role you slip into naturally even when it doesn't serve you. What is it?
usageNotes: Works well for leaders, creatives, and anyone in a team dynamic.

text: Notice which feedback you've received most consistently across multiple jobs or collaborators. The pattern is pointing at something.
usageNotes: Good for performance review prep or recurring feedback loops.

text: If your career were a conversation, what's the sentence you keep almost saying but pulling back?
usageNotes: Metaphorical — good for users with strong inner voice but low external expression.

text: At this crossroads — stay, stretch, or leave — which option terrifies you least? That one might be the answer.
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors based on user's chosen direction.

text: Who in your life do you perform for — and when did the performance begin?
usageNotes: Deep relational prompt. Best for users in therapy or doing inner work.

text: Notice who you text when something good happens versus who you call when something breaks. Those two lists reveal different kinds of love.
usageNotes: Low-resistance entry point into attachment style awareness.

text: There's a relationship in your life that runs on an old script — one that both people keep performing past its expiration date. Who is it with?
usageNotes: Good for examining family of origin dynamics or long-term friendships.

text: You described a relationship tension in a previous session. Has the tension moved — or are you still in the same position?
usageNotes: Requires prior session data on relationship themes.

text: Today, say something true to someone close to you that you've been softening into something smaller.
usageNotes: Intimacy and authenticity challenge — gentle but direct.

text: Think of the person you saw or spoke to most recently. Did your body open or close in their presence? That response knows something.
usageNotes: Somatic relational awareness. Works for any close relationship.

text: Name one relationship you want to invest in this week — not fix, not manage, just genuinely be present for.
usageNotes: Positive forward-facing relationship intention.

text: In conflict, are you more likely to go cold, go loud, or go away? Which one costs you the most?
usageNotes: AI tailors response based on conflict style the user identifies.

text: What do you need from people that you've never directly asked for?
usageNotes: Surfaces unspoken relational needs — works well with attachment style work.

text: Notice which relationships leave you feeling more like yourself, and which ones leave you feeling like a slightly edited version.
usageNotes: Self-expansion vs. self-contraction framework in relationships.

text: You have a type — not romantically, but the kind of person you keep choosing as a close friend. What does that person always seem to have or be?
usageNotes: Friendship pattern recognition. Works for all relationship types.

text: Reach out to one person today with no agenda — just to say you've been thinking of them. Notice the impulse to add more. Don't.
usageNotes: Connection micro-practice. Good for people who over-function relationally.

text: When a relationship starts to feel like work, is your first instinct to try harder — or to quietly begin to let go?
usageNotes: AI tailors based on whether user identifies as an over-pursuer or withdrawer.

text: What version of you shows up in intimacy that almost no one else sees?
usageNotes: Vulnerability and intimacy depth prompt.

text: Think of the last argument you had. Where did it actually start — the surface moment, or something older underneath?
usageNotes: Conflict origin tracing. Good for couples and close friendships.

text: You named a relational pattern you wanted to shift in a past session. Have you caught yourself in it since — or have you started to move around it?
usageNotes: Tracks behavioral change in relationship context.

text: This week, notice once when you're tempted to manage someone else's emotions instead of sitting with your own.
usageNotes: Emotional over-responsibility awareness — good for helpers and fixers.

text: The relationship that taught you the most about yourself wasn't necessarily the easiest one. What did it teach you?
usageNotes: Meaning-making from difficult relationships.

text: Notice who gets the most polished version of you versus who gets the real-time, unedited one. What determines that line?
usageNotes: Safety and authenticity gradient in relationships.

text: What are you still carrying from a relationship that ended — not grief, but something it taught you to do that you haven't unlearned yet?
usageNotes: Post-relationship pattern tracing. Works for romantic and non-romantic relationships.

text: Let someone be wrong today without correcting them. Notice what it costs you, and what it frees.
usageNotes: Letting go of control in relationships. Good for high-functioning, opinionated users.

text: Which feels riskier to you: needing someone fully, or being fully needed?
usageNotes: AI tailors response based on which the user selects — vulnerability direction.

text: Think about your most important relationship right now. If that relationship had a weather system, what would today's forecast be?
usageNotes: Atmospheric, low-pressure relational check-in.

text: Choose one relationship to give the gift of full presence to this week — phone down, pace slowed. Just that one.
usageNotes: Presence-over-productivity relational intention.

text: Somewhere in your family history, someone loved the way they'd been taught to love — not the way they'd have chosen. Are you repeating that?
usageNotes: Intergenerational love patterns. Deep — use with sufficient context.

text: What's the apology you've been waiting for that you may never receive — and what would it unlock in you if it arrived?
usageNotes: Forgiveness and unresolved relational wounds. Handle with care.

text: Today, notice when you use humor to deflect tenderness in a conversation. It happens faster than you think.
usageNotes: Defense mechanism awareness — humor as armor.

text: You reflected on someone you've been distancing from. Has the distance felt protective or costly since then?
usageNotes: Requires prior session data on relationship avoidance.

text: Ask someone a question today that you already think you know the answer to. Listen as if you don't.
usageNotes: Curiosity and assumption-breaking in relationships.

text: If you could change one pattern in how you love — not fix someone else, just shift something in yourself — what would it be?
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors response to user's named pattern.

text: What belief about yourself have you updated in the last year that you used to hold as permanent?
usageNotes: Identity evolution prompt. Best for reflective users with some self-awareness baseline.

text: Notice which habits you call 'discipline' and which ones you call 'struggle.' They might be doing the same work.
usageNotes: Reframes self-judgment around consistency.

text: The thing you're trying to become has a shadow version — a way it could calcify into rigidity. Can you name both?
usageNotes: Dialectical thinking about growth goals — prevents single-axis self-improvement.

text: Look at the growth intention you set in a previous session. Have you been moving toward it, away from it, or orbiting it?
usageNotes: Requires prior session data. Tracks growth intention momentum.

text: Do one small thing today that the old version of you would have skipped. It doesn't have to be big — it just has to be real.
usageNotes: Behavioral identity reinforcement through micro-actions.

text: Right now, in your body — do you feel like someone becoming something, or someone holding on? Neither is wrong. Both are useful.
usageNotes: Growth-state somatic check-in.

text: Set one identity intention for this week — not a task, but a way of being. State it as: 'This week, I am someone who...'
usageNotes: Identity-based goal framing (James Clear style). Best Monday use.

text: Which has driven your growth more: pain that pushed you, or desire that pulled you?
usageNotes: AI tailors response based on user's primary growth motivator.

text: What's something you've gotten better at that no one around you has noticed yet?
usageNotes: Surfaces quiet inner growth invisible to social validation.

text: Today, catch yourself the moment you default to an old story about who you are. Just catch it — no need to rewrite it yet.
usageNotes: First-step identity awareness exercise.

text: Growth often stalls not from laziness but from the terror of leaving a familiar self behind. What familiar self are you still protecting?
usageNotes: Self-protection vs. self-expansion. Good for users in plateau.

text: Track your actual behavior — not your intentions — in one small area for the next 24 hours. The gap between the two is your real curriculum.
usageNotes: Intention-behavior gap awareness. Works with any habit domain.

text: The next version of yourself is already forming. Do you feel mostly excited, mostly scared, or mostly uncertain about who they'll be?
usageNotes: AI adjusts tone based on emotional orientation toward future self.

text: What have you forgiven yourself for that took longer than it should have — and what made the forgiveness finally arrive?
usageNotes: Self-forgiveness as growth mechanism.

text: On a scale from depleted to full — where is your growth energy today? Growth requires fuel. Name what's in your tank.
usageNotes: Practical readiness check before growth-oriented action.

text: You described a version of yourself you were working toward. Has that image gotten clearer, blurrier, or completely changed?
usageNotes: Future-self tracking over multiple sessions.

text: This week, instead of trying to add a new habit, remove one thing that's been quietly shrinking you. Name it.
usageNotes: Subtraction as growth — less is sometimes the lever.

text: You've been measuring your growth by the wrong metric. What would a truer metric look like?
usageNotes: Useful for achievers who track outputs but miss internal shifts.

text: Notice what you do with your discomfort today — do you push through it, translate it, or move away from it? All three are strategies. Only one of them learns.
usageNotes: Discomfort as growth signal — behavioral observation.

text: What role has failure played in making you who you are — and is that role still welcome?
usageNotes: Relationship with failure as teacher vs. enemy.

text: Say something kind about yourself today — specifically, about who you're becoming, not who you were.
usageNotes: Self-compassion micro-practice. Good for inner critics.

text: Is your current growth pace: too fast (unsustainable), too slow (frustrating), or surprisingly right?
usageNotes: AI tailors response and framing based on pace the user reports.

text: Where in your body do you feel the weight of who you haven't yet become? Where do you feel the lightness of who you already are?
usageNotes: Somatic duality — aspiration and acceptance in the body.

text: What will you know about yourself by this time next month that you don't quite know yet? Set the question, not the answer.
usageNotes: Holding questions open as a growth practice.

text: You have a growth ceiling — something invisible that keeps you from going further. You probably already know what it is. Say it.
usageNotes: Ceiling-breaking prompt. Works well in coaching contexts.

text: You named a habit you wanted to shift. How many days have actually passed since you last practiced it? Be honest — the number is not a verdict.
usageNotes: Non-judgmental habit tracking with callback to prior session.

text: Notice how you talk about yourself to others today — is it humble, deflecting, accurate, or performing a story?
usageNotes: Self-narrative in social contexts.

text: What would the most grown version of you do today that you're currently not doing?
usageNotes: Future-self bridge — behavioral aspiration.

text: Write down one thing you want to release from your identity — a label, a story, a limitation — and sit with the space that opens when you do.
usageNotes: Identity subtraction exercise. Best in quiet reflection time.

text: At this point in your evolution, what deserves the most energy: more becoming, more accepting, or more integrating what you've already learned?
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors based on which growth mode resonates most.

text: Before the day takes you anywhere, scan your body from crown to feet. Where are you holding tension you haven't named yet?
usageNotes: Morning somatic grounding. Best first prompt of the day.

text: Notice what kind of morning this is — a slow one, a jagged one, or one that already feels like it's getting away from you. That texture matters.
usageNotes: Tonal entry point into daily rhythm awareness.

text: What depletes you most reliably — people, tasks, environments, or your own internal weather?
usageNotes: Energy drain categorization. Good for energy management planning.

text: Before the day gets loud, name one thing you want to protect — a mood, a boundary, or a stretch of uninterrupted time.
usageNotes: Protective intention-setting. Classic morning ritual prompt.

text: You have a reliable energy crash window — a time of day when you consistently fade. You probably know exactly when it is. What do you do with it?
usageNotes: Ultradian rhythm awareness. Works well with productivity optimization.

text: Today feels like: (a) a high-energy day with capacity to spare, (b) a functional but flat day, or (c) a low day that needs careful handling. Which one?
usageNotes: AI tailors the day's guidance and energy management advice to user's selection.

text: Today, match your hardest task to your best hour — not the hour that's convenient, the hour that's actually sharp.
usageNotes: Chronobiology-aligned task scheduling micro-practice.

text: You described your typical energy rhythm in a previous session. Has the pattern held, shifted, or surprised you this week?
usageNotes: Tracks energy pattern changes over time.

text: Pay attention today to what feeds you without costing anything — small things: a particular light, a quiet minute, a movement your body enjoys.
usageNotes: Micro-restoration awareness. Good for high-output, low-rest users.

text: Is your tiredness today physical, emotional, social, or existential? They all require different remedies.
usageNotes: Fatigue differentiation — prevents misdiagnosis of low energy.

text: Rate your current mental bandwidth from 1–10. Now rate your physical energy. Now rate your emotional availability. Where's the biggest gap?
usageNotes: Tripartite energy assessment. Good for identifying imbalance.

text: Name one thing you'll say no to today in order to say yes to your energy. Not a big no — just one.
usageNotes: Energy conservation via selective commitment.

text: You push through the dip instead of resting in it — and then wonder why you crash harder later. What would it cost you to stop at the dip, just once?
usageNotes: Rest resistance pattern interrupt. Works for high-achievers and workaholics.

text: When you're running on low, you tend to: push harder, withdraw completely, or get irritable before you notice you're tired. Which pattern is most you?
usageNotes: AI tailors depletion recovery advice based on user's identified low-energy coping style.

text: Take one deliberate pause today — not a break that fills with phone scrolling, but an actual pause. Two minutes, nothing.
usageNotes: Real rest micro-challenge. Good for users in perpetual input mode.

text: Notice what your body has been asking for in the past week that you've been ignoring. Sleep, movement, stillness, food, air — one of them is overdue.
usageNotes: Body wisdom listening practice.

text: What are you currently powering with willpower that used to be powered by genuine desire?
usageNotes: Motivation depletion signal. Works well for burnout early warning.

text: You identified a depletion pattern in a previous session. Is it still running — or have you interrupted it at all?
usageNotes: Depletion pattern tracking across sessions.

text: Without judgment — what does your body most want right now? Move toward it by even 10 percent.
usageNotes: Body-need honoring without full commitment. Low-bar entry point.

text: What's the one environment change today — lighting, sound, space, company — that would most protect your energy? Make it.
usageNotes: Environmental energy design — actionable and immediate.

text: Your body has been signaling something for weeks and you've been translating it as 'just stress.' What if it's not stress — what if it's direction?
usageNotes: Body signal reframing — signals as information, not noise.

text: Track the moment today when your energy shifts — up or down. What triggered it? Context, conversation, thought, or something physical?
usageNotes: Real-time energy trigger tracking. Good for awareness-building.

text: Does rest feel earned to you, or do you feel you need to justify it before you're allowed to take it?
usageNotes: AI tailors based on user's rest relationship — guilt-based vs. integrated.

text: If your energy were a natural resource, how would you describe its current state: abundant, seasonal, or close to depletion?
usageNotes: Metaphorical energy assessment. Works well for creative thinkers.

text: Move your body for five minutes today in a way that has nothing to do with fitness — just pure physical pleasure or expression.
usageNotes: Joyful movement vs. obligatory movement distinction.

text: You set an energy protection intention in a previous session. Did you actually honor it — or did the day swallow it whole?
usageNotes: Accountability loop for energy intentions.

text: This evening, before sleep — what one thing will you do to genuinely close the day instead of just letting it trail off?
usageNotes: Evening wind-down ritual. Best used in afternoon or evening session.

text: You've been treating rest as recovery from work instead of fuel for living. What would your relationship with rest look like if you reversed that?
usageNotes: Rest philosophy reframe. Good for productivity-obsessed users.

text: Scan your emotional register right now — not what you think you should feel, but what's actually moving through you. Name it without fixing it.
usageNotes: Emotional energy labeling without resolution pressure.

text: If you had to redesign one part of your daily rhythm to match your actual energy — not your ideal or your obligations — what would change first?
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors based on user's identified rhythm misalignment.

text: When did you last make something with no audience in mind — no one to show, no outcome attached? How long ago was that?
usageNotes: Pure creation vs. performed creation distinction.

text: Notice what stops you mid-idea — the self-editor, the inner critic, the practical voice. It has a very specific face. What does it look like?
usageNotes: Inner critic personalization for creative work.

text: Your creative blocks are shaped like something — procrastination, perfectionism, busyness, fear of the blank page. Which shape is yours today?
usageNotes: Block taxonomy — specific framing prevents generic advice.

text: In a previous session you named a creative project or idea you'd been sitting on. Is it still alive — or have you let it quietly expire?
usageNotes: Creative idea momentum tracking over sessions.

text: Make something today that takes under ten minutes and has no practical use. The point is the making, not the thing.
usageNotes: Anti-productivity creative micro-act. Good for overthinking creatives.

text: Where is your creative energy sitting right now — in your chest, hands, head, gut? Follow it to where it wants to go.
usageNotes: Somatic entry point into creative work.

text: Name one creative act you'll protect time for this week — no matter how small, no matter how imperfect.
usageNotes: Creative time protection intention. Monday-morning or week-start prompt.

text: Right now, your creative life feels more like: a drought, a trickle, a steady stream, or a flood you can't channel. Which one?
usageNotes: AI tailors creative support based on user's current flow state.

text: What creative identity did you carry as a child that you've quietly outgrown — or quietly abandoned?
usageNotes: Childhood creative self recovery. Good for disconnected or blocked creatives.

text: Notice what you consume obsessively right now — art, content, music, stories. It's probably adjacent to something you want to make.
usageNotes: Input-output creative inference. Good for discovery mode users.

text: You've been waiting for the right conditions to create. The conditions are never coming. What would you begin today under imperfect conditions?
usageNotes: Perfectionism interrupt. Works well for long-term procrastinators.

text: Steal twenty minutes today and fill them with whatever you've been calling 'one day.' Do it badly. Do it anyway.
usageNotes: 'Done is better than perfect' activation micro-challenge.

text: Do you tend to abandon creative work because of self-doubt, distraction, or an actual change of interest? Knowing the difference changes what you do next.
usageNotes: AI tailors next-step advice based on abandonment pattern user identifies.

text: What are you most afraid people would see if your creative work were fully honest?
usageNotes: Vulnerability and exposure in creative work. Deep — use thoughtfully.

text: Before you start creating today, check in: are you in receptive mode (open, curious) or closed mode (tight, self-judging)? The mode shapes the work.
usageNotes: Creative state awareness before beginning work.

text: You described a creative block in a previous session. Has the block shifted form, dissolved slightly, or hardened since then?
usageNotes: Tracks creative block evolution over time.

text: What's one creative idea or impulse you'll follow this week without explaining or justifying it to anyone first?
usageNotes: Permission-free creative action. Good for people who over-explain.

text: Your inner critic and your inner artist are the same age — they both grew up in the same house. Who do you feed more?
usageNotes: Internal creative dialectic — critic vs. artist as equal forces.

text: Notice what you're drawn to aesthetically right now — color, texture, sound, space. Your sensory preferences are always pointing somewhere.
usageNotes: Aesthetic intelligence as creative compass.

text: If you could only make one thing in the world — one piece, one project, one act of creation — what would it be?
usageNotes: Core creative identity distillation. Works well as a deeper session prompt.

text: Find one unexpected thing today that makes you stop and pay attention for no practical reason. That attention is the beginning of everything.
usageNotes: Wonder and attention as creative practice.

text: Is your biggest creative challenge right now about starting, sustaining, or finishing? Each one has a completely different solution.
usageNotes: AI tailors advice to user's identified creative stage challenge.

text: Your creative energy has a temperature right now — warm, cold, or running hot. What does that temperature tell you about what to work on today?
usageNotes: Thermal metaphor for creative state assessment.

text: Set one creative constraint for today: a time limit, a medium, a rule. Constraints don't kill creativity — they give it edges to push against.
usageNotes: Constraint-as-catalyst. Good for creatives who need structure.

text: Somewhere between your first idea and the finished thing, you lose the energy. Which step is the one where you consistently disappear?
usageNotes: Creative process gap identification.

text: You named a creative identity in an earlier session. Is that identity still how you'd describe yourself — or does it need updating?
usageNotes: Creative identity evolution tracking.

text: Watch yourself today for the moment when you dismiss a creative impulse as too small, too weird, or too late. That's the one worth following.
usageNotes: Recapturing dismissed creative impulses.

text: What would you create if you weren't afraid of being derivative, ordinary, or misunderstood?
usageNotes: Permission expansion by naming specific creative fears.

text: Write down three ideas you've had in the past week that you didn't act on. One of them is probably worth revisiting.
usageNotes: Idea retrieval and selection micro-exercise.

text: Right now your creative life needs: more permission, more discipline, more solitude, or more courage. Which one is actually true?
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors based on user's identified creative need.

text: What was the first money message you received as a child — spoken or unspoken — and is it still running your decisions?
usageNotes: Money origin story. Best for financial therapy or early deep dive.

text: Notice how you feel the moment before you make a purchase — any purchase. Anxious, numb, excited, guilty? That emotion is your money story in real time.
usageNotes: Emotional pre-purchase awareness. Good for spending pattern work.

text: You have a financial ceiling — an internal number above which money stops feeling comfortable. Do you know what it is?
usageNotes: Upper limit problem in finances. Works for earners who sabotage growth.

text: You identified a money belief in a previous session. Has that belief loosened at all — or does it still feel like a fact?
usageNotes: Tracks money belief rigidity over time.

text: Today, spend money on something that represents who you're becoming — not who you've been. Even if it's small.
usageNotes: Intentional spending as identity investment.

text: Think about your financial situation right now. Does it feel like something you're managing, something that's managing you, or something you're slowly building?
usageNotes: Agency vs. victimhood framing in financial self-perception.

text: Name one financial conversation you need to have this month that you've been avoiding. Not next year — this month.
usageNotes: Financial avoidance interrupt. Works for partners, business, or solo.

text: When you think about money, your default mode is: scarcity (never enough), sufficiency (just enough), or abundance (plenty). Which one runs deepest?
usageNotes: AI tailors financial mindset coaching based on user's dominant money orientation.

text: What does financial security feel like in your body — and have you ever actually felt it, even briefly?
usageNotes: Somatic financial safety — connects cognitive and felt sense of security.

text: Track one financial decision you make today — however small. Notice whether it comes from clarity, anxiety, habit, or desire.
usageNotes: Real-time financial decision motive awareness.

text: You spend money on your image, your comfort, your family, your future — but rarely on your growth. Is that true? What would change if it weren't?
usageNotes: Investment in self vs. external categories. Good for under-investors.

text: Look at one financial number today you've been avoiding — a balance, a statement, a number you've been keeping at arm's length.
usageNotes: Financial avoidance micro-confrontation.

text: Earning more money would most change: your sense of freedom, your sense of safety, your sense of power, or your sense of worth. Which one first?
usageNotes: AI tailors response based on user's primary money-as-symbol belief.

text: What financial choice are you most proud of — and what does it reveal about your relationship with money at its best?
usageNotes: Positive financial identity reinforcement.

text: How much of your mental bandwidth is currently occupied by financial worry — a little, a lot, or almost constantly? What does that number tell you?
usageNotes: Financial cognitive load assessment.

text: You named a financial goal in a previous session. Have you moved toward it — or has the goal quietly shifted while you weren't looking?
usageNotes: Financial goal tracking across sessions.

text: Name one financial intention for this month that reflects what you value — not what you've been told you should do with money.
usageNotes: Values-aligned financial planning. Good for month-start.

text: The person who taught you about money didn't teach you the whole truth — they taught you their own fear or luck dressed up as wisdom. What did they pass on?
usageNotes: Intergenerational money programming. Deep — pair with financial therapy work.

text: Notice who you compare your finances to most frequently. Is that comparison motivating you — or quietly diminishing you?
usageNotes: Financial social comparison and its psychological effects.

text: If money were not an object, what would you do more of — and what does that tell you about where meaning lives for you?
usageNotes: Money-as-constraint reverse reveal. Good for values clarity.

text: Write down what you want your financial life to look like in three years — not vaguely, specifically. If you can't say it, you can't build toward it.
usageNotes: Financial vision specificity. Starts with clarity, not tactics.

text: Your financial anxiety tends to spike around: income uncertainty, unexpected expenses, comparison to others, or the feeling that you'll never catch up. Which one is most true?
usageNotes: AI tailors financial anxiety management advice to user's primary trigger.

text: Open your bank account right now and check your balance — then notice what you feel. That feeling is a chapter of your money story.
usageNotes: Direct financial engagement with emotional awareness.

text: This week, make one money decision that feels aligned — not optimal, not perfect, just honest. Name it.
usageNotes: Values-alignment in weekly financial decision-making.

text: You've been spending money to manage emotions you haven't dealt with. Name the emotion that has the biggest tab.
usageNotes: Emotional spending pattern reveal. Handle sensitively.

text: You reflected on your money story once before. What one thing still feels unresolved — a belief, a fear, a behavior you keep cycling back to?
usageNotes: Deep financial pattern tracking. Requires prior session context.

text: Notice what 'enough' feels like today — is it a number, a feeling, or something that keeps moving away as you get closer?
usageNotes: Sufficiency vs. hedonic adaptation in financial wellbeing.

text: Where in your financial life are you playing small — not because of real limits, but because of an old belief about what you deserve?
usageNotes: Worth and deservingness in financial behavior.

text: Talk about money directly with one person today — what you have, what you want, what you're working toward. Money shrinks its grip when you say it out loud.
usageNotes: Money taboo-breaking micro-practice.

text: If your financial life could only improve in one way — more income, better relationship with spending, less anxiety, or clearer long-term vision — which one would change the most?
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors strategic financial insight to user's primary need.

text: What's the thing in your head right now that's taking up the most space — and how long has it been living there?
usageNotes: Cognitive inventory opener. Best as a morning or mid-day check-in.

text: Notice today how many open loops you're holding — things you started, things you promised, things you're waiting on. Don't solve them. Just count them.
usageNotes: Zeigarnik effect awareness. Counting without resolving — just noticing.

text: The cognitive overhead of being the person who remembers everything for everyone has a cost. When did you last put that cost down?
usageNotes: Mental load as relational labor — good for primary household managers.

text: You identified a major mental load in a previous session. Has anything been offloaded, delegated, or resolved — or has more been added on top?
usageNotes: Mental load tracking and change monitoring across sessions.

text: Write down every open task that's living in your head right now. Put them in a list, anywhere — notes, paper, voice memo. Get them out of RAM.
usageNotes: Brain dump micro-challenge. Immediate cognitive relief practice.

text: How much of your available mental bandwidth is genuinely free right now — not occupied, not on standby, just open? Estimate the percentage.
usageNotes: Cognitive capacity estimation — prompts honest load awareness.

text: This week, choose one thing you're currently holding that you could delegate, defer, or delete. Name it and name which of the three you'll do.
usageNotes: 3D framework: delegate, defer, delete. Practical cognitive relief.

text: Your mental load is heaviest in: work obligations, household management, emotional caretaking, or your own internal rumination. Which category is the main weight?
usageNotes: AI tailors mental load reduction strategies to user's heaviest category.

text: What are you holding for someone else that they could be holding for themselves?
usageNotes: Over-functioning and relational load-bearing pattern.

text: Track your mental switching today — how many times do you pivot between contexts, topics, or concerns? That number has a cost your focus is paying.
usageNotes: Context-switching awareness as cognitive tax.

text: You are most overwhelmed not when there is more to do, but when you can't see which thing matters most. What's the thing that matters most right now?
usageNotes: Cognitive overwhelm as prioritization deficit — reframe and focus.

text: For one hour today, close every open tab — literal and mental. Work only on the one thing in front of you. Notice how much you resist that.
usageNotes: Single-tasking and attention reclamation practice.

text: When your mental load peaks, do you: freeze and do nothing, frantically start everything, or pick one thing and tunnel into it?
usageNotes: AI tailors cognitive overload coping advice based on user's overwhelm response.

text: What worry has been looping in the background of your mind for more than a week — and what would it take to finally close that loop?
usageNotes: Chronic rumination identification and resolution path.

text: What does your mind feel like right now: a cluttered desk, a clear surface, a filing system, or a drawer that won't close? What's in the drawer?
usageNotes: Spatial metaphor for cognitive state assessment.

text: You named a cognitive offloading strategy in a previous session. Are you using it — and if not, what got in the way?
usageNotes: Accountability loop for cognitive management strategy adoption.

text: Name one decision you've been deferring that's taking up mental space every time you avoid it. Set a deadline to decide by end of week.
usageNotes: Deferred decision mental overhead reduction.

text: Your mental load doubles when you're trying to anticipate everyone else's needs. Who else in your life could be anticipating their own?
usageNotes: Anticipatory labor and relational boundary work.

text: Notice today how often your attention drifts to the future or the past. Where it lands most often is where your mental weight lives.
usageNotes: Temporal focus awareness — past/future orientation as load indicator.

text: What would you think about if you weren't always thinking about all of this?
usageNotes: Cognitive liberation inquiry — what lies beneath the noise.

text: Take one item off your mental list today — not by doing it, but by deciding it doesn't need to be done. Notice the relief.
usageNotes: Decision by deletion — undone tasks as cognitive freedom.

text: Your mental clutter comes mostly from: unfinished tasks, unresolved relationships, unanswered questions, or unrealized plans. Which type dominates?
usageNotes: AI tailors cognitive clearing strategy to user's dominant clutter type.

text: On a scale of 1–10, how full is your head right now? What's in the top three slots?
usageNotes: Quick mental occupancy check — useful for daily habit building.

text: Create one mental container this week — a designated time to think about one specific category of worry, and nowhere else.
usageNotes: Worry time technique as cognitive load management.

text: Overwhelm is not always about volume — sometimes it's about the absence of trust that it will all work out. Which is your overwhelm?
usageNotes: Volume vs. control overwhelm — different roots, different remedies.

text: Last time you mapped your mental load, you named what you were most tired of carrying. Is it still there — lighter, heavier, or gone?
usageNotes: Longitudinal mental load change tracking.

text: Notice which conversations you're having in your head that you haven't yet had out loud. Each unspoken conversation is a background process running.
usageNotes: Mental simulation vs. real communication gap.

text: What does your mind do at 3am that it won't do in daylight? That content is trying to find a way through.
usageNotes: Nocturnal rumination as unprocessed mental load.

text: Before bed tonight, spend five minutes offloading everything on your mind onto paper or a voice note. Give your brain explicit permission to let go.
usageNotes: Evening brain dump as sleep and cognitive hygiene practice.

text: To meaningfully reduce your mental load, you need most: better systems, better boundaries, better support, or better permission to let things be imperfect. Which one?
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors cognitive relief strategy to user's identified bottleneck.

text: What do you most want your child — or the children in your life — to remember about who you were when they were young?
usageNotes: Legacy-forward parenting reflection. Works for biological and non-biological parent figures.

text: Notice today when you are fully present with your child — and when you're physically there but mentally elsewhere. Both moments are telling.
usageNotes: Presence quality over quantity. Good for distracted or overworked parents.

text: You are parenting partly from your wounds and partly from your wisdom. Today, which one is at the wheel?
usageNotes: Triggered vs. intentional parenting distinction.

text: You named a parenting pattern you wanted to shift in a previous session. Have you caught it, interrupted it, or repeated it since then?
usageNotes: Tracks behavioral change in parenting context.

text: Do one thing today that you would have wanted a parent to do for you. Give it to someone younger, smaller, or less certain.
usageNotes: Reparenting through forward action. Intergenerational healing practice.

text: How full is your parenting tank today — not your love, but your patience, your capacity, your presence? What would refill it?
usageNotes: Parenting capacity vs. parenting love distinction.

text: Name one value you want to actively pass on this week — not through a speech, but through how you show up.
usageNotes: Values transmission through modeling rather than telling.

text: Your biggest parenting fear is: repeating your own parent's mistakes, being too permissive, being too controlling, or not being enough. Which one?
usageNotes: AI tailors reassurance and reflection to user's primary parenting fear.

text: Which patterns from your own childhood are you consciously breaking — and which ones slipped through anyway?
usageNotes: Intergenerational pattern interruption and honest audit.

text: Watch how your child handles frustration today. Somewhere in that response is a mirror of something they've learned — from you, or despite you.
usageNotes: Child behavior as parenting feedback loop — nonjudgmental observation.

text: You parent the way you do partly because of what you lacked. But what if you're overcompensating in a direction that has its own cost?
usageNotes: Overcompensation in parenting — pendulum awareness.

text: Have one conversation today with a child in your life where you ask more than you tell. Let their perspective surprise you.
usageNotes: Listening-forward parenting. Good for directive or anxious parents.

text: When you think about legacy — what you'll leave behind — is it more about: your values, your relationships, your achievements, or the world you helped build?
usageNotes: AI tailors legacy exploration based on user's primary inheritance frame.

text: What do you wish someone had told you before you became a parent — and who in your life right now could use that same truth?
usageNotes: Wisdom-forward parenting reflection with community extension.

text: Think about the last time you lost your temper or your patience with a child. What was actually underneath that moment — what were you carrying before it started?
usageNotes: Parental reactivity tracing — underlying state not the trigger.

text: You named a value you wanted to pass on in a previous session. Have you lived that value visibly enough for the children around you to see it?
usageNotes: Values transmission behavioral follow-through tracking.

text: This week, create one moment of deliberate connection — not scheduled, not structured, just fully present with a child who matters to you.
usageNotes: Unstructured presence intention. Works against over-programmed family life.

text: The story your child is building about themselves is happening right now, partly through you. What story do you think they're writing?
usageNotes: Child's emerging self-narrative as parenting mirror.

text: Notice today what your child does when they're not performing for you — at play, at rest, in a moment you weren't supposed to see. That's who they are.
usageNotes: Authentic child self vs. performed self observation.

text: What do you model about how to handle difficulty — and would you want your child to handle it exactly the same way?
usageNotes: Difficulty modeling as parenting behavior audit.

text: Apologize to your child today for something — even something small. Model that adults are wrong sometimes and repair is always possible.
usageNotes: Parental repair modeling. Teaches accountability through demonstration.

text: The thing you most want your child to develop is: resilience, empathy, confidence, or curiosity. Which one are you most actively cultivating?
usageNotes: AI tailors parenting reflection to user's identified developmental priority.

text: How does your relationship with your own parents live in your body today — as warmth, tension, grief, gratitude, or something unresolved?
usageNotes: Intergenerational somatic inheritance — parents-of-origin body mapping.

text: Choose one thing this month to do for your own wellbeing — not as a sacrifice, but as modeling that adults matter too.
usageNotes: Parental self-care as visible modeling. Counters martyrdom parenting.

text: Somewhere in your parenting, you are unconsciously recreating a dynamic from childhood — not because it worked, but because it's the only map you had.
usageNotes: Unconscious family script replication. Deep — use in extended reflection.

text: You described a quality of presence you wanted to bring to parenting. Has your presence matched that intention since then?
usageNotes: Parenting presence intention accountability.

text: Notice how your child seeks your attention today — what they do to find you, what face they make when they find you. That's the data point.
usageNotes: Attachment behavior as relational feedback in parenting.

text: What part of yourself are you hoping your child will carry forward — not as a burden, but as a gift?
usageNotes: Positive legacy transmission — strength-forward parenting.

text: Tell someone younger than you one true thing about your life — not a lesson, just a true thing. Let them decide what to do with it.
usageNotes: Authentic intergenerational storytelling vs. instructional parenting.

text: If you could leave one single thing behind — one thing you want to be remembered for in the lives of children — what would it be?
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors legacy reflection based on user's named inheritance.

text: What quality in others irritates you most consistently? That irritation is often a map to something unacknowledged in yourself.
usageNotes: Classic projection-based shadow entry point. Works for users with some Jungian awareness.

text: Notice today when you feel a flash of envy. Don't dismiss it — envy is specific. It always points toward something you want and haven't claimed.
usageNotes: Envy as desire signal — shadow-to-aspiration reframe.

text: The thing you judge most harshly in others — are you absolutely certain it's not somewhere in you, just dressed differently?
usageNotes: Judgment as shadow mirror. Requires tolerance for discomfort.

text: In a previous session you named something you'd been avoiding looking at directly. Have you looked yet — or have you built a more elaborate avoidance?
usageNotes: Avoidance escalation tracking in shadow work.

text: Name one thing about yourself that you would be embarrassed for people to know. You don't have to share it — just stop hiding it from yourself.
usageNotes: Private self-acknowledgment. First step of shadow integration.

text: Where in your body does your shadow live? Shame tends to sit low. Rage tends to sit high. Fear tends to hollows you. What are you carrying, and where?
usageNotes: Somatic shadow mapping. Works well in meditative or calm state.

text: This week, make one choice that the 'good' version of you would make — and notice what the shadow self says in protest. That protest is useful data.
usageNotes: Shadow voice as behavioral data — dialectical action.

text: Your shadow most often shows up as: self-sabotage, people-pleasing, aggression, or withdrawal. Which is your most frequent costume?
usageNotes: AI tailors shadow work exploration based on user's identified shadow expression.

text: What do you want that you've never admitted wanting — not because you don't want it, but because wanting it felt wrong or dangerous?
usageNotes: Forbidden desire in shadow work — reclaiming exiled wants.

text: Notice today when your reaction to a situation seems larger than the situation warrants. That disproportionate charge is the shadow speaking.
usageNotes: Emotional disproportionality as shadow indicator.

text: Your avoidance patterns have a very specific shape — places you reliably don't go, thoughts you reliably don't finish. What's the shape of yours?
usageNotes: Avoidance cartography — mapping the territory of the unseen.

text: Think of someone you judge harshly. Now write one sentence that explains — not excuses — how they got there. Compassion is a shadow cure.
usageNotes: Compassion as projection antidote. Shadow work through radical understanding.

text: The fear you most avoid naming directly is: fear of failure, fear of being seen, fear of being ordinary, or fear of being too much. Which one?
usageNotes: AI tailors shadow fear exploration based on user's primary unnamed fear.

text: What version of yourself have you been exiling — too loud, too needy, too angry, too sad, too ambitious? What would happen if you let them back in?
usageNotes: Exiled self-integration — Jungian shadow reclamation.

text: Right now, is your shadow active or quiet? When it's quiet, it's often because you're performing well. When it's loud, what is it responding to?
usageNotes: Shadow activation state awareness — context-dependent behavior.

text: You surfaced a shadow element in a previous session. Have you been able to acknowledge it without collapsing into it or running from it?
usageNotes: Shadow integration progress — neither fusion nor avoidance.

text: Name one behavior this week you want to observe in yourself — not fix, just observe with curiosity instead of shame.
usageNotes: Witnessing vs. judging shadow behavior — compassionate observation.

text: The part of you that gets in your own way has logic. It developed for a reason. Do you know its original purpose?
usageNotes: Shadow origin tracing — protection logic of the saboteur self.

text: Notice today what you hide in plain sight — the version of you that shows up in private that you'd never present publicly. What does it do differently?
usageNotes: Public vs. private self gap as shadow terrain.

text: What is the most honest thing you could say about yourself that you've never said to anyone?
usageNotes: Radical self-honesty prompt. Best for deep, extended session.

text: Today, catch yourself using humor, busyness, or helpfulness to avoid something uncomfortable. Just catch it — that awareness is the work.
usageNotes: Defense mechanism behavioral catch in real-time.

text: When your shadow runs the show, does it tend to harm: yourself, your relationships, your work, or your sense of self? Which costs the most?
usageNotes: AI tailors shadow impact work based on user's primary domain of shadow expression.

text: What emotion are you most reluctant to feel right now — not avoid, just feel, fully, for sixty seconds? Name it without going into it.
usageNotes: Naming without immersion — shadow emotion identification.

text: This week, practice being honest in one small situation where your instinct would be to soften, deflect, or omit. Notice the specific moment you hedge.
usageNotes: Shadow work through honesty micro-practice.

text: Your golden self — the polished, performing, well-regarded version — has a shadow self of equal size. What does that shadow self want that the golden self won't allow?
usageNotes: Persona-shadow duality — gold and shadow as inseparable pair.

text: You identified an avoidance pattern last time. Has it become more visible — do you catch it sooner now, or does it still have a head start on you?
usageNotes: Avoidance pattern detection speed tracking — metacognitive growth.

text: Notice today when you feel small — deflated, overlooked, unimportant. That feeling isn't weakness. It's information about an unmet need.
usageNotes: Smallness as need indicator — shadow in its contracted form.

text: What are you most afraid people would discover about your inner life if they could see it clearly for one unguarded minute?
usageNotes: Core exposure fear in shadow work. Handle with compassion.

text: Write a one-paragraph letter to your shadow self — not to fix or fight it, but to acknowledge it. Just say: I see you, I know you're here, you're not going anywhere.
usageNotes: Shadow acknowledgment practice. Core of Jungian integration work.

text: What would change in your life if you stopped being afraid of your own shadow — if you could hold it with the same compassion you offer to everyone else?
usageNotes: End-of-theme synthesis. AI tailors based on user's named shadow and self-compassion capacity.

text: What decision have you been making in slow motion for months — extending the timeline because the answer feels too consequential?
usageNotes: Decision avoidance via timeline extension. Good for big-life-decision users.

text: Notice today when you feel certain versus when you feel clear. Certainty and clarity are not the same thing — one closes, one opens.
usageNotes: Epistemological distinction — certainty vs. clarity in decision-making.

text: You have a decision style — you either decide fast and regret later, or decide slow and regret the delay. Which are you, honestly?
usageNotes: Decision style self-knowledge — fast vs. slow decision-maker.

text: You reflected on a decision you were facing in a past session. Have you made it, deferred it, or discovered it wasn't the real decision after all?
usageNotes: Decision outcome or evolution tracking across sessions.

text: Make one small, reversible decision today that you've been overthinking. It matters less which choice you make than that you make it.
usageNotes: Decision muscle-building via low-stakes action. Counters analysis paralysis.

text: When you imagine making the decision you've been avoiding, where does your body react? Expand or contract? That's your most honest vote.
usageNotes: Somatic decision-making — body as decision instrument.

text: Name one priority this week that you'll protect from being diluted by urgency that isn't actually important.
usageNotes: Important vs. urgent differentiation in weekly priority-setting.

text: When facing a hard decision, do you trust: your gut first, your analysis first, the advice of others first, or what the best version of you would do?
usageNotes: AI tailors decision-making guidance based on user's primary decision instrument.

text: What decision from the past year are you most at peace with — and what made that peace possible?
usageNotes: Positive decision reinforcement — learning from aligned choices.

text: Track today how often you say 'I should' versus 'I want' versus 'I've decided.' The verbs reveal your relationship with your own agency.
usageNotes: Language of agency vs. obligation in daily decision framing.

text: You're not struggling to decide — you already know. The struggle is accepting the cost of the answer you already have.
usageNotes: Known decision vs. accepted cost distinction — clarity vs. courage.

text: Write down your top three values right now. Then look at the decision you've been avoiding and ask: which value wins if I choose this?
usageNotes: Values-hierarchy decision framework. Simple but clarifying.

text: Clarity for you tends to come from: sleeping on it, talking it out, writing it out, or suddenly in a completely unrelated moment. Which way does it arrive?
usageNotes: AI tailors clarity cultivation practice to user's natural clarity channel.

text: Which of your current commitments — if you're honest — no longer aligns with who you're becoming?
usageNotes: Commitment audit against evolving identity. Works for career and personal pivots.

text: Think of your most important current priority. Now ask: is the energy behind that priority fresh, or are you running on the fumes of an old why?
usageNotes: Motivation currency check — fresh vs. stale 'why' behind priorities.

text: You named a core value in a previous session. Look at last week's decisions — how many of them were actually in service of that value?
usageNotes: Values-behavior gap tracking across sessions.

text: Before the week's noise begins, name the single most important thing you need to move forward on — and commit to one action toward it today.
usageNotes: Weekly single-priority focus and first-step commitment.

text: You optimize for the option that keeps the most doors open — but eventually, choosing everything means choosing nothing. What door are you most afraid to close?
usageNotes: Optionality addiction as decision avoidance. Good for multi-potentialites.

text: Notice today when you defer to what someone else wants instead of what you want. How much of your day is actually yours?
usageNotes: Agency audit in daily micro-decisions.

text: If you had to choose between safety and meaning today — really had to choose — which would you reach for first?
usageNotes: Safety-meaning axis in values-based decision-making.

text: Take one decision off your plate today by delegating it — not the important ones, just one that someone else could make just as well.
usageNotes: Decision fatigue reduction through delegation practice.

text: When you've made a bad decision in the past, what was most often true: you didn't have enough information, you ignored what you already knew, or you let others decide for you?
usageNotes: AI tailors decision-quality advice based on user's primary past error pattern.

text: Right now, are you operating from a place of clarity, confusion, or a kind of forced certainty that masks unresolved doubt? All three require different moves.
usageNotes: Clarity state self-diagnosis — different states require different decision approaches.

text: Name one thing this month you'll decide on — fully, irreversibly, without reopening — and then stop spending energy on alternatives.
usageNotes: Decision finalization and commitment as cognitive relief.

text: Clarity doesn't always arrive before the decision. Sometimes it arrives because of the decision. What would you learn by choosing, even now?
usageNotes: Action-before-clarity reframe. Works for over-analyzers.

text: The last time you described feeling truly clear — do you know what produced that clarity? Can you recreate the conditions?
usageNotes: Clarity conditions reverse-engineering from prior session data.

text: Notice today the gap between what you say matters and where you actually put your time. That gap is your most honest priority statement.
usageNotes: Espoused values vs. enacted values gap — time as truth.

text: What would you do differently if you trusted your own judgment more completely — not more arrogantly, just more honestly?
usageNotes: Self-trust and self-reliance in decision-making.

text: Make one decision today based entirely on your values — no ROI analysis, no consensus-building, no fear of judgment. Just: what do I actually believe?
usageNotes: Values-only decision practice. Strips away noise.

text: You've arrived at the end of a long reflection. What one thing are you most clear about now that you weren't before? Name it, write it, keep it.
usageNotes: Final synthesis prompt for the full 300-day journey. AI tailors based on user's named clarity.
